title: Needle-felted snail
subtitle: A garden snail with a spiral shell in merino
excerpt: Build a rounded shell coil and a soft grey body from merino roving using needle-felting. The finished snail is about 6 cm long and makes an appealing shelf ornament or nature-table piece for children.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British needle-felting workshop practice. Snail forms are a staple beginner project in UK garden-creature felting series.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry method of sculpting wool by stabbing repeatedly with barbed needles. The barbs catch and tangle the wool fibres with each pass, building up a firm, shaped mass without water.

content.text: This snail is made in two pieces: a coiled shell and a flat body. The shell is a roll of merino wound into a dome and needled to hold its spiral shape. The body is a flat oval with a rounded head and two small horn tendrils. Both pieces are felted separately and then joined. Use a warm brown, terracotta, or tan merino for the shell and grey or beige for the body.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees into the foam pad only. Keep a finger guard on the supporting hand.

content.text: Pull a long, thin rope of brown merino (about 40 cm long) and roll it tightly around itself, starting from one end, to make a flat coil about 4 cm in diameter. Needle the coil flat on the mat to hold the spiral shape. Then curve the coil gradually upward from the centre to form a dome. Needle the dome shape from all sides, working inward from the top to compact the centre. Spend about 10 minutes total on the shell until it is firm and dome-shaped.

Form 6 g of grey merino into a flat oval about 7 cm long and 3 cm wide. Needle it on the mat until it holds its shape and is about 8 mm thick. Round the head end and narrow the tail end very slightly.
Step 3: Add the head and horns

Roll a small ball of grey merino about 1.5 cm across and needle it to the rounded head end of the body, blending the join smoothly. For the horns, roll two thin cylinders of grey merino about 1.5 cm long each, needle them to firm points, and attach them to the top of the head about 1 cm apart.
Step 4: Attach the shell and add eyes

Place the shell on top of the body, centred and slightly toward the rear. Needle the base of the shell firmly into the body surface through several passes. The join should not be visible from the side. Add two tiny black dots at the tips of the horns for eyes using the size-40 needle and a small wisp of dark merino.
Go over the whole piece with the size-40 needle to smooth the surface. This
finishing pass removes any surface fuzz and gives the body and shell a consistent texture.

items.problem: The shell loses its dome shape during felting and flattens
items.cause: Needling pressure was applied from above rather than from the sides as well
items.fix: Work the needle in from the sides at a shallow angle while rotating the dome on the mat. Side pressure supports the dome height as the fibres consolidate.

items.problem: The horns bend to one side after attaching
items.cause: The horns were felted while still too soft
items.fix: Needle the horns more firmly on the mat before attaching them. They should hold their upright position without support before being joined to the head.
